main_summary.summary: Walter Bruce, born in the Rolling Wall community of rural Holmes County, Mississippi, was interviewed by John Dittmer in Durant on March 11, 2013, for the Library of Congress / Smithsonian Civil Rights History Project. Bruce describes a sharecropping childhood as the youngest of 14 children, his work as a self-taught carpenter who built houses across Holmes and surrounding counties, and his founding of a gospel singing group that toured regionally and remained active for more than 54 years. He recounts entering civil rights organizing in the early 1960s through mass meetings in the Mileston community, his role alongside SNCC field workers, and the Holmes County Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) organization. Bruce details a Lexington jailhouse arrest after a J.J. Young death-in-custody boycott, his service as MFDP coordinator across 17-18 community meeting sites, the 1967 election that sent Robert Clark to the Mississippi state legislature, the firebombing/shooting attacks on Wednesday-night meetings, and his appointment to the Durant city school board after pushing for desegregation. Bruce identifies himself as still the chair of the Holmes County Freedom Democratic Party at the time of recording.
key_themes: Holmes County Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party organizing
Mileston voter registration and the first 14 applicants
Gospel music and the Black church tradition
Rural school desegregation and the Durant school board
main_summary.historical_significance:
Bruce is a first-person witness to the Holmes County Movement, one of the most successful rural Black organizing efforts of the 1960s Mississippi civil rights struggle. He names the Mileston 14 voter-registration applicants (including Hartman Turnbow and Ralph Hayse), describes J.J. Young's death in the Durant jail after authorities allegedly withheld heart medication, and recounts the 1967 county-wide election that sent Robert Clark to the Mississippi state legislature. The interview also documents the founding of one of the early rural Black-run community health clinics in the United States, descended from the Mileston community center that he helped build with Sue and Henry Lorenzi.

Bruce's testimony establishes the procedural mechanics of MFDP organizing at the community level: third-Sunday county-wide meetings, candidate selection requirements tied to community-meeting attendance, and inter-community coordination across 17-18 precincts. As an artisan-organizer (carpenter, gospel singer, FDP chair) rather than a clergyman or teacher, his account also complicates the standard narrative that the Black professional class led local organizing in the rural South.

chapters.title: Rolling Wall Childhood
chapters.summary: Bruce describes his birth in the Rolling Wall community of rural Holmes County and growing up as the youngest of 14 children in a sharecropping family. He recounts walking miles to a small wooden Methodist church, briefly working cotton fields, and an early childhood incident in which he confronted a white man over an impounded animal.
related_events: Rural Mississippi childhood under Jim Crow

chapters.title: Carpentry and Gospel Group
chapters.summary: Bruce describes his self-taught carpentry trade, which he calls a gift from God, building homes across Holmes and surrounding counties with a small crew. He details the gospel singing group he founded that toured Leflore County, Greenwood, Chicago, and beyond, broadcasting on local radio and continuing for more than 54 years.
related_events: Black gospel touring circuit in Mississippi

chapters.title: Entering the Movement
chapters.summary: Bruce describes weighing his carpentry business against civil rights involvement before deciding 'what good is a job, you ain't got no freedom.' He recalls hearing about Emmett Till's lynching, meeting Medgar Evers and Aaron Henry, and traveling to Greenwood for movement meetings as SNCC workers organized in Holmes County.

chapters.title: Freedom Summer and the Mileston 14
chapters.summary: Bruce recounts Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers staying with local Black families, the literacy-test obstacles at the courthouse (including how many bubbles in a bucket of water), and the April 1963 attempt by 14 Holmes County Black residents to register to vote. He names Hartman Turnbow, Ralph Hayse, Russell Whitaker, James Bourne, and Alma Mitchell Carnegie among the applicants who lived in Mileston and owned their own land.

chapters.title: J.J. Young and the Lexington March
chapters.summary: Bruce describes the death of J.J. Young in the Durant jail after authorities allegedly refused him heart medication. He recounts inspecting the body, organizing a boycott and march to the courthouse in Lexington, his own arrest with 12 others, and the subsequent crowd that filled the Lexington square so densely that police could not drive through it.

chapters.title: MFDP and Atlantic City
chapters.summary: Bruce details his election as chair of the Holmes County Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, a role he says he still held at the time of the interview. He describes his work as MFDP coordinator across 17-18 community meetings, supporting the 1964 challenge at the Atlantic City National Democratic Convention, and the 1965 effort to seat Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray in Congress.

chapters.title: Sue and Henry Lorenzi and Healthcare
chapters.summary: Bruce describes the five-year presence of Sue and Henry Lorenzi as white SNCC volunteers in Holmes County and their role in building the Mileston community center. He recalls Dr. Brock as a rural physician who would visit Black homes, the absence of Black doctors in the county, segregated waiting rooms, and the Mileston-area community health clinic that helped found the federal community health center program.
related_events: Mound Bayou and Mileston community health center movement

chapters.title: Durant School Board and Downtown Desegregation
chapters.summary: Bruce describes Mayor Levin, a white Durant lawyer who supported integrating the city school board, and Bruce's appointment alongside Mr. D.F. (Owen) as the first two Black members. He recounts integrating a Durant cafe after the 1964 Civil Rights Act, removing the segregation signs at the ice train station, and a boycott of a grocery store that fired its Black worker after a fight with a white worker.

chapters.title: Carroll County Crisis and School Integration
chapters.summary: Bruce describes being summoned to a Carroll County school after a Black student was accused of raping a white teacher's daughter, his confrontation with white sheriffs, and his investigation that revealed the two were in a consensual relationship. He then discusses the aftermath of Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education (1969), as white families moved their children to private 'Christian' academies while public schools became overwhelmingly Black.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education (1969)

chapters.title: Self-Defense at the Meeting Hall
chapters.summary: Bruce describes weekly Wednesday-night meetings at a country building where white attackers in cars repeatedly shot up the roof. He recounts organizing armed defenders on both sides of the road, who returned fire above the attackers' car the next Wednesday, and an arson attempt at a nearby church (described as Saigon Pagoda or Mt. Pisgah) where attackers had soaked a rope in gasoline to set fire to the building.

chapters.title: Robert Clark and the 1967 Election
chapters.summary: Bruce describes the 1966 congressional run by Ralph Hayse and the 1967 county election that produced Robert Clark as the first African American in the Mississippi state legislature in the 20th century. He explains the decision to run an independent slate in the general election rather than the primary, the third-Sunday county-wide candidate-selection meetings, and the role of Rev. Eddie Clark and Dr. Wilbert McLean.

chapters.title: Reflection on Family and Integration
chapters.summary: Bruce reflects on his wife's support across decades of late-night meetings, his refusal to take money from his organizing work, and his conviction that 'we don't need an all-Black or all-white' political coalition. He affirms that the Holmes County Freedom Democratic Party remains active and that the county is still organized around third-Sunday meetings.
